step_by_step_thinking: I agree with agent1’s assessment. Reed-Sternberg cells indeed originate from mature B-cells through specific genetic alterations that transform normal B-cells into these large multinucleated tumor cells. This transformation results in the acquisition of unique phenotypic markers such as abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli—features distinctly associated with B-cell lineage malignancies rather than those found in macrophages, natural killer cells, or T cells.
raw_response:
I agree with agent1’s assessment. Reed-Sternberg cells indeed originate from mature B-cells through specific genetic alterations that transform normal B-cells into these large multinucleated tumor cells. This transformation results in the acquisition of unique phenotypic markers such as abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli—features distinctly associated with B-cell lineage malignancies rather than those found in macrophages, natural killer cells, or T cells.

Answer: D. Based on the provided information and clinical understanding, the correct derivation source for Reed-Sternberg cells remains B cells.
